text: The surgeon performs a robotic rectopexy on an elderly female patient with full thickness rectal prolapse. The procedure involves posterior, right lateral, and anterior mobilization with suture proctopexy. The surgeon frees adhesions of the proximal sigmoid colon in the deep pelvis, unfolding a loop stuck to the rectum. The rectosigmoid mesentery is stretched over the sacral promontory to address redundancy.

The surgeon stretches the recto sigmoid mesentery over the sacral promontory, carefully preserving the hypogastric plexus and nerves during the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. The camera is port-hopped from port three to port two to improve the view and prevent the right robotic arm from contacting the iliac vessels on the sidewall. The setup now includes two right arms and one left, facilitating access to the deep pelvis.

The surgeon navigates the redundancy of the sigmoid colon to achieve a clear view, utilizing pneumoperitoneum to delineate the dissection plane. The hypogastric plexus and nerves are visible and preserved during the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. The robotic right arm occasionally contacts the iliac vessels on the sidewall, prompting a camera port switch from port three to port two for improved central visualization of the sacral promontory. The setup now includes two right arms and one left arm, optimizing access to the deep pelvis with scissors in arm three.

The surgeon suspends a flap of the bladder using a nylon Keith needle to improve visibility of the anterior cul de sac. The anterior dissection is performed in the rectovaginal septum, with the vagina visible. The surgeon palpates both the rectum and vagina to ensure the dissection is as low as intended. The dissection continues laterally and down to the pelvic floor, completing a total mesorectal excision (TME) in the posterior, right lateral, and anterior regions. The sacral promontory is fully exposed, and vessels are cauterized directly on the bone to prevent bleeding during needle insertion.

The surgeon suspends a flap of the bladder using a nylon Keith needle to improve visibility of the anterior cul de sac. The anterior dissection is performed in the rectal vaginal septum, with the vagina visible. The surgeon palpates both the rectum and vagina to ensure the dissection is as low as intended. The dissection continues laterally and down to the pelvic floor, completing a total mesorectal excision (TME) in the posterior, right lateral, and anterior regions. The sacral promontory is fully exposed, and vessels are cauterized directly on the bone to prevent bleeding during needle insertion.

The surgeon takes large bites of the periosteum with silk sutures, securing the lateral stalk. The procedure involves two bites to the periosteum, performed as outpatient surgery. The surgeon closes the peritoneal space to prevent small bowel from entering the pelvis, taking small bites of the peritoneum while being cautious of proximity to the right ureter and iliac vessels.

The surgeon takes large bites of the periosteum with a silk suture, securing the lateral stalk. The procedure involves careful suturing to prevent small bowel from entering the dissected pelvic space, with small bites taken from the peritoneum. The surgeon is cautious of proximity to the right ureter and iliac vessels during closure.

The surgeon sutures the anterior peritoneal flap from the dissected rectovaginal septum to the suture line of the closure of the peritoneal flap, using two sutures to secure the anterior component. The visual result appears satisfactory, with the redundancy of the rectosigmoid mesentery on stretch over the sacral promontory.

The surgeon sutures the anterior peritoneal flap from the dissected rectovaginal septum to the suture line of the closure of the peritoneal flap, using two sutures to secure the anterior component. The sutured area appears visually satisfactory.
